title: The Shift from Task-Specific to Generalists
concept: Previously, we designed custom neural nets for each task (NER, sentiment, classification); transfer learning lets one generalist model solve all of them via fine-tuning.

exercises.prompt: What is the modern trend in AI development for tasks-prompt:

options.text: Building more highly-specialized models from scratch
Using generalist models that can be adapted to many tasks
Moving away from neural networks entirely

exercises.explain: Historically, AI models were specialists built for one task. Today's trend is generalist models that can adapt to many tasks.

cards.title: A Jack of All Trades
cards.body: Not long ago, if you wanted an AI to read medical records and another to write emails, you had to build and train two completely different neural networks from scratch. What if one brain could learn to do both with just a tiny bit of extra practice?

cards.title: What is a Generalist Model?
cards.body: In the past, we built separate custom neural networks for every single task, like sentiment analysis or named entity recognition (NER). Now, with **transfer learning**, we train one massive **generalist model** on huge amounts of text, and then slightly tweak (fine-tune) it to solve all of these different tasks.

cards.title: The Household Generalist
cards.body: Imagine hiring a separate specialist for every household chore—one person to only wash spoons, another to only dust bookshelves, and a third to only sweep. It's expensive and slow. Instead, you hire a smart generalist housekeeper. With just a few quick instructions (fine-tuning), they can easily handle the dishes, dusting, and sweeping all on their own!

cards.title: One Model, Many Jobs
cards.body: Instead of building "Sentiment-Bot 3000" and "Grammar-Bot 5000" separately, developers now download a single general model like BERT or GPT. By showing it just a few examples of target data, the same core model learns to label parts of speech, detect angry emails, or translate languages.

cards.title: Myth: Generalists Know It All
cards.body: Many people think generalist models are perfect at everything right out of the box. In reality, while they have broad knowledge, they still need a small phase of targeted training called 'fine-tuning' to master specific, niche tasks.

cards.title: The Evolution of AI Workflow
cards.body: Comparing the old way of building AI with the modern generalist approach.

left.text: Separate custom models built and trained from scratch for NER, sentiment, and translation.

right.text: One massive base model adapted to all tasks through quick fine-tuning.

exercises.prompt: Before transfer learning became popular, how did developers handle different language tasks like sentiment analysis and entity recognition?

options.text: We had to build and train a brand new, custom neural network from scratch.
We used a single model that automatically knew how to do everything.
We did not use neural networks at all for language tasks.
Models were shared instantly without needing any training data.

exercises.explain: Previously, NLP developers had to design, code, and train completely unique neural network architectures for every new problem they wanted to solve.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Transfer learning allows us to reuse the same generalist model for multiple different tasks instead of building new ones from scratch.
exercises.explain: Transfer learning allows a single base model to be fine-tuned for many different tasks, meaning we don't have to start from scratch every time.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: Instead of building a model from scratch, we adapt a generalist model to a new task using a process called __________.
exercises.explain: Transfer learning allows us to take a pre-trained generalist model and adapt it to a specific task using a quick process called fine-tuning.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents the shift to generalist AI models?

options.text: Building a single-purpose neural network that only detects spam emails.
Using a pre-trained language model and adapting it to classify sentiment, extract names, and translate text.
Writing a long list of manual rules to find keywords in a document.

exercises.explain: Option A describes a task-specific model built for only one job. Option B describes the modern generalist approach where one base model is adapted for multiple tasks. Option C is just manual programming.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best summarizes the modern shift from task-specific AI to generalist models?

options.text: Building and training separate, isolated models from scratch for every single new task.
Starting with one pre-trained generalist model and fine-tuning it for multiple different tasks.
Deleting old models and letting the user write rules by hand instead of using neural networks.
Using transfer learning to force a model to only perform one single mathematical operation forever.

exercises.explain: Historically, building AI meant starting from scratch for every new task. Today, we train one giant generalist model on massive amounts of data first, and then easily fine-tune it to handle sentiment analysis, translation, translation, or entity recognition.
